questions.question: Explain the difference between shared and private variables in OpenMP. Under what circumstances does a variable become shared by default?
keyPoints: Variables with static storage duration (globals/file-scope) are shared by default
Heap-allocated memory is shared by default
Automatic variables (stack-allocated in enclosing scope) are shared in parallel regions
Variables declared within the parallel construct are private
Shared variables have single memory location accessed by all threads
Private variables have thread-local copies without guaranteed initialization

questions.question: What is a data race in OpenMP? State the formal condition for a data race according to the OpenMP specification.
keyPoints: Data race: concurrent access to same memory location with at least one write
No explicit synchronization orders the accesses
Result is non-deterministic and undefined
OpenMP spec defines synchronization through barriers, locks, atomic operations
Any two operations form a race if they conflict (one write or both writes) and are not ordered

questions.question: Describe the purpose and behavior of the 'firstprivate' and 'lastprivate' clauses in OpenMP with suitable examples.
keyPoints: firstprivate: initializes private copies with master's initial value
lastprivate: copies value from last iteration/section back to original variable
Useful in iterative algorithms where initial and final values matter
Example: computing Fibonacci where each iteration depends on previous values
lastprivate only guarantees correct value from last iteration in sequential order, not necessarily last to execute

questions.question: Explain the 'reduction' clause in OpenMP. How does it prevent race conditions? What is the identity element for different reduction operators?
keyPoints: reduction creates private copy for each thread initialized to identity value
Each thread performs local computation on private copy
At end, all private copies are combined using the reduction operator
Prevents race conditions by ensuring no concurrent writes to shared variable
+ has identity 0, * has identity 1, min has identity largest, max has identity smallest
Operators: +, -, *, &, |, ^, &&, ||, min, max

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'default(none)' in OpenMP? Why is it considered a best practice in parallel programming?
keyPoints: Forces explicit declaration of data-sharing attribute for every variable
Prevents accidental sharing leading to subtle race conditions
Improves code readability and maintainability
Acts as defensive programming practice
Requires programmer to consciously decide sharing attribute
Makes data dependencies explicit and easier to review

questions.question: Differentiate between 'private' and 'threadprivate' in OpenMP with respect to variable lifetime and usage context.
keyPoints: private: creates uninitialized copies for each parallel region
threadprivate: preserves variable value across multiple parallel regions in same thread
threadprivate applicable only to file-scope or static variables
private applicable to any variable in parallel region
threadprivate similar to thread-local storage in pthreads
private is reinitialized each parallel region; threadprivate persists
RACE CONDITION 1: sum += arr[i] has concurrent read-modify-write on shared variable sum
RACE CONDITION 2: arr[i] = arr[i] * 2 has concurrent read-modify-write on shared array arr
FIX 1 for sum: Use reduction(+:sum) clause
FIX 2 for arr: Use private(arr) to create local copy, or use firstprivate(arr) if initial values needed, or use atomic for each update
Alternative: Partition work differently and do local accumulation before combining
Analysis: Without fixes, sum will be less than actual sum (lost updates) and arr values unpredictable
Corrected code uses reduction(+:sum) and private(i) is implicit for loop index
Output with fix: 500500 (sum of 1 to 1000)
Matrix A and B: READ-ONLY during computation, should be SHARED (efficient - single copy in memory)
Matrix C: WRITE by each thread for assigned iterations, should be PRIVATE for local computation then written back
Loop indices i, j, k: PRIVATE (each thread needs own copy for iteration)
Variable temp: PRIVATE (accumulator for each (i,j) pair)
private(i,j,k,temp) ensures thread-local copies
Alternative: lastprivate(j,k) if final values needed after region
Performance consideration: A[i][k] access pattern may cause false sharing in inner loop
False sharing mitigation: Consider tiling/blocking or firstprivate for inner loop indices
No race condition on C[i][j] because each (i,j) pair is assigned to exactly one thread in row-wise partition

questions.question: Write a detailed note on the OpenMP data-sharing attribute rules. Include a comparison table of all data-sharing attributes, their syntax, and appropriate use cases.
keyPoints: OpenMP data-sharing rules define implicit behavior based on variable scope
Variables declared outside parallel region: shared by default (with exceptions)
Variables declared inside parallel region: private by default
Loop indices in parallel for: private by default
Class variables accessed in parallel: shared if pointing to shared data
Table columns: Attribute, Syntax, Initialization, Persistence, Use Case
SHARED: shared(var), single memory location, all threads access, accumulation results
PRIVATE: private(var), uninitialized, per parallel region, loop indices, temporary computation
FIRSTPRIVATE: firstprivate(var), initialized from master, per parallel region, initial value needed
LASTPRIVATE: lastprivate(var), uninitialized, copy from last iteration, sequential final value needed
REDUCTION: reduction(op:var), initialized to identity, combine at end, associative operators
THREADPRYNAMIC: threadprivate(var), persists, across regions, thread-local state
DEFAULT: default(none/shared), influences implicit behavior, defensive programming
Special rules for tasks: captured variables are firstprivate by default
